sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 25-year-old male with a 3-week history of right upper quadrant abdominal pain, described as a severe squeezing pain that worsens with eating, especially fatty foods.
The pain is located beneath the right ribcage and is accompanied by nausea and occasional low-grade fever.
The patient reports that the pain is an 8 out of 10 in severity.
The pain is exacerbated by eating, particularly fatty foods, and the patient has noticed some improvement when avoiding these foods.
The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes, which he has managed with diet and exercise, and has lost 25 pounds in the past 3 months.
He reports that his blood sugars have improved, but the abdominal pain persists.
Past medical history: The patient has type 2 diabetes, for which he has managed with diet and exercise.
He also has a family history of gallbladder disease, as both his parents had their gallbladders removed.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional low-grade fever and nausea, but no vomiting.
He denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: Not mentioned.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 128/88, respiratory rate is 16, pulse rate is 68, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
The patient is afebrile.
Physical exam: The patient has positive bowel sounds, a soft and nondistended abdomen, and slight guarding to the right upper quadrant.
Murphy's sign is positive, but no peritoneal signs are appreciated.
Test Results: The abdominal ultrasound shows multiple gallstones in the gallbladder, mild thickening of the gallbladder wall, and a normal common bile duct size of 4.1 mm.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has mild cholecystitis with gallstones.
Problem list: Cholecystitis with gallstones; Gallstones; Type 2 diabetes (active).
Plan:
I prescribed pain management as needed, and I recommended a laparoscopic cholecystectomy to remove the gallbladder.
I will perform the surgery in the next week, and the patient will spend the night in the hospital for post-procedure monitoring.
The patient should avoid high-fat foods and eat foods high in fiber after surgery.
I also advised the patient to follow up with his primary care provider to monitor his type 2 diabetes.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in a week for post-operative care, and then will be released to resume normal activities, including hiking and nature photography.
